attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of worsening right knee pain. The pain began after a twisting injury while gardening. Initially, he managed with paracetamol and ibuprofen, but the pain has progressively worsened and he now feels generally unwell. He has never had similar episodes. His medical history includes prostate cancer in remission, hypertension, and atrial fibrillation. His medications include perindopril and metoprolol.

On examination, his pulse rate is 104/min, blood pressure 137/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 38.1°C. The right knee shows generalised swelling and erythema. There is marked warmth and tenderness of the entire knee with dramatically reduced range of motion.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Joint aspiration for microscopy and culture
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suspicious for septic arthritis including fever, joint pain, swelling, and markedly reduced range of motion. While the history of trauma may suggest a mechanical cause, the progressive worsening and systemic symptoms point towards infection. Joint aspiration for microscopy and culture is essential to confirm the diagnosis and guide antimicrobial therapy. The most common causative organism is Staphylococcus aureus. Synovial fluid analysis can provide immediate information through cell count and Gram stain, allowing for rapid decision-making regarding admission and antibiotics.

Blood cultures and empiric antibiotics would be appropriate after joint aspiration, but obtaining synovial fluid for culture should take precedence. Starting antibiotics before obtaining cultures may reduce the likelihood of identifying the causative organism. Additionally, blood cultures are positive in only about 50% of septic arthritis cases, making synovial fluid essential for diagnosis.

While an urgent orthopaedic consultation will likely be needed, this should not delay obtaining synovial fluid for analysis. The orthopaedic team will require the results of joint aspiration to guide their management decisions, including the potential need for surgical washout. Delaying aspiration while waiting for the orthopaedic review could worsen outcomes.

Knee X-ray and inflammatory markers may provide supportive information but would not confirm the diagnosis or guide specific antimicrobial therapy. X-rays are often normal early in the course of septic arthritis, and while elevated inflammatory markers support the diagnosis, they are neither sensitive nor specific enough to confirm it. These investigations can be performed concurrently with joint aspiration but should not delay it.

Knee MRI would provide detailed imaging of joint structures but would delay the diagnosis and treatment of septic arthritis, which requires urgent intervention. While MRI is highly sensitive for joint inflammation, it is time-consuming, expensive, and unnecessary for initial diagnosis when clinical features strongly suggest infection. MRI might be considered later to assess for complications such as osteomyelitis or if the diagnosis remains unclear after initial investigation.

The mortality rate of septic arthritis can be as high as 11%, and delays in diagnosis and treatment can lead to permanent joint damage. Therefore, prompt joint aspiration is crucial in cases where clinical features suggest infection.
learningPoints: Joint aspiration is essential to confirm septic arthritis and guide antimicrobial therapy
Fever, joint pain and reduced range of motion are classic features of septic arthritis
Risk factors for septic arthritis include age over 60 years and recent joint trauma
references: Long B, Koyfman A, Gottlieb M. Evaluation and management of septic arthritis and its mimics in the emergency department. Western Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2019 Mar;20(2):331.
